enrichment.short_description: Galene is a free and open-source, self-hosted WebRTC videoconferencing server (a Selective Forwarding Unit) built in Go, designed to run multi-party video meetings, lectures and conferences on modest server hardware.
enrichment.seo_title: Galene Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Galene is a free, self-hosted WebRTC video server (SFU) built in Go. Explore its features, MIT license, architecture, pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Galene is a self-hosted videoconferencing server that falls into the category of software known as a Selective Forwarding Unit, or SFU: rather than mixing audio and video streams on the server the way an MCU does, it forwards individual WebRTC streams from each participant to the others, which keeps CPU usage low while still allowing many-to-many video calls. The project was started in the spring of 2020 by Juliusz Chroboczek, a researcher at IRIF (the computer science research lab of Universite Paris Cite), during the first COVID-19 lockdown in France. The immediate motivation was practical rather than commercial: the Department of Computer Science needed a way to run lectures, tutorials and even remote examinations online, and existing options at the time were either proprietary, resource-hungry, or both. By December 2020 the software had matured enough to be released as free and open-source software under the MIT licence, and it has been in continuous production use at Universite Paris Cite and Sorbonne Universite ever since, alongside appearances at academic and community events such as SOCS 2020, JFLA 2021 and LibrePlanet 2024. Architecturally, Galene's server is written in Go and built on the Pion implementation of WebRTC, a pure-Go media stack. This gives Galene excellent portability: the server runs on Linux (amd64, arm64 and armv7), on OpenWRT-class MIPS routers, on macOS and on Windows, and it ships as a single, dependency-light binary that is simple to deploy without containers or complex orchestration. The default web client is plain JavaScript and works in all current desktop and mobile browsers without a plugin, while a native Android client is also available for users who want screen sharing and a more integrated mobile experience. On the media side, Galene supports the VP8, VP9, H.264 and AV1 video codecs, simulcast and Scalable Video Coding (SVC) for adaptive quality under constrained bandwidth, a built-in TURN server for NAT traversal, server-side recording, background blur in the client, peer-to-peer file transfer between participants, and support for the WHIP ingest protocol so external encoders and streaming tools can publish directly into a Galene group. An administrative HTTP API allows operators to script the creation and management of groups and user accounts, important for institutions that need to provision many course sections or meeting spaces automatically. Resource efficiency is a defining characteristic and a large part of why Galene has found a niche among self-hosters, universities and privacy-conscious organizations. According to the project's documentation, a typical 100-person lecture requires only about a quarter of a CPU core, and the software can sustain roughly 300 simultaneous participants per core in a one-to-many pattern. Many-to-many meetings, where every participant sends and receives every other participant's video, scale quadratically rather than linearly, so Galene's practical ceiling there is closer to about 20 participants per core, a limitation shared by essentially every SFU-based system rather than something unique to Galene. Because Galene is free, open-source software rather than a company, there is no subscription, no per-seat pricing and no vendor lock-in: anyone can download the source from GitHub, compile or run the provided binary, and operate their own instance indefinitely at zero licensing cost. Development has been supported at different points by Nexedi (between September 2020 and February 2021) and, since June 2024, by the NLnet Foundation's NGI Zero Core programme, which funds public-interest internet infrastructure. The codebase has also undergone an independent security review by Radically Open Security, funded through NLnet, a meaningful trust signal for institutions considering it for sensitive use cases like exams or confidential meetings. Galene's natural competitors are other self-hostable, open-source videoconferencing systems, most notably Jitsi Meet and BigBlueButton, and to a lesser extent OpenVidu and custom mediasoup-based deployments. Compared with Jitsi, which is the most widely deployed alternative and backed by 8x8, Galene is deliberately smaller in scope: it does not attempt to be a full-featured meeting product with breakout rooms, calendar integrations, or a polished commercial UI, and instead focuses on being lightweight, auditable and easy to run on a single low-cost server. This makes it attractive to system administrators at universities, hackerspaces, NGOs and free-software-aligned organizations who want a videoconferencing tool they can fully understand and control, but it is a poorer fit for organizations wanting a managed, support-backed SaaS product with enterprise features like SSO federation or compliance certifications, since there is no company behind Galene to sell them. The target audience, in short, is technically capable self-hosters: university IT departments running lecture and exam infrastructure, free-software communities and conferences, small-to-medium organizations that already run their own servers for privacy or cost reasons, and individual power users comfortable with command-line deployment. It is less suited to non-technical end users looking for a one-click hosted meeting link, since standing up a Galene instance requires basic server administration, DNS and TLS configuration, and capacity planning around the CPU-per-participant guidance above. For the audience it does serve, Galene offers a rare combination in the videoconferencing space: genuinely low resource requirements, a permissive licence, funded development, and a track record of real production use at scale in academic settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Galene is a free, open-source videoconferencing server built as a Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU), meaning it relays WebRTC audio and video streams between participants rather than mixing them, which keeps server load low for multi-party calls.

Created in 2020 by researcher Juliusz Chroboczek at IRIF (Universite Paris Cite) to support remote lectures and exams during the COVID-19 lockdown, Galene has since been used in production at Universite Paris Cite, Sorbonne Universite, and events like LibrePlanet 2024.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Galene supports VP8, VP9, H.264 and AV1 codecs with simulcast and Scalable Video Coding, a built-in TURN server, server-side recording, screen sharing, background blur, peer-to-peer file transfer and WHIP protocol ingest.
An administrative HTTP API lets operators script group and user provisioning, while a native Android client and full browser support cover desktop and mobile use cases without requiring any plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Galene is entirely free under the MIT licence; there are no subscription tiers, seat fees, or usage limits when self-hosted, and the source code can be modified freely, including for commercial deployments.
The only real costs are server hardware and administrator time. Ongoing development is funded through public-interest programs such as NLnet's NGI Zero Core rather than customer revenue.

features.title: Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU) architecture
features.description: Forwards WebRTC streams between participants without server-side mixing, keeping CPU load low for multi-party calls.

features.title: Multi-codec video support
features.description: Supports VP8, VP9, H.264 and AV1 with simulcast and Scalable Video Coding for adaptive quality under constrained bandwidth.

features.title: Built-in TURN server
features.description: Includes NAT traversal out of the box, removing the need for a separate external TURN service in most deployments.

features.title: Session recording
features.description: Supports server-side recording of meetings for later playback.

features.title: WHIP ingest protocol
features.description: Lets external encoders and streaming software publish video directly into a Galene group.

features.title: Administrative HTTP API
features.description: Allows scripted creation and management of groups and user accounts for large-scale provisioning.

features.title: Native Android client
features.description: Dedicated mobile app with screen sharing, alongside full browser support on desktop and mobile.

features.title: Peer-to-peer file transfer and background blur
features.description: Built-in file sharing between participants plus client-side background blur for privacy.

pros: Free and MIT-licensed, with full source code access and no vendor lock-in
Very low CPU and RAM footprint compared to many comparable SFUs
Cross-platform server support across Linux, macOS, Windows and ARM devices
Actively maintained with funded development and an independent security review
Proven in production at major universities and open-source conferences
cons: No official managed or hosted SaaS option; self-hosting is required
Requires system administration skills to deploy and maintain
User interface is more minimal than commercial tools like Zoom or Google Meet
Many-to-many meetings scale quadratically, limiting very large all-camera calls
Smaller community and fewer integrations than Jitsi Meet

faqs.question: Is Galene free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Galene is MIT-licensed free and open-source software with no cost to download, run, or modify.

faqs.question: Who maintains Galene?
faqs.answer: It is primarily maintained by Juliusz Chroboczek at IRIF, University of Paris Cite, with development currently funded by the NLnet Foundation.

faqs.question: How many participants can Galene handle?
faqs.answer: As a rough guide, Galene supports about 300 participants per CPU core in one-to-many (lecture) scenarios and about 20 participants per core in many-to-many meetings.

faqs.question: Does Galene have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a native Android client with screen sharing; iOS and other devices can join through a mobile browser.

faqs.question: How does Galene compare to Jitsi Meet?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source SFUs, but Galene is lighter-weight and simpler to deploy on modest hardware, while Jitsi has a larger ecosystem and more polished feature set.

faqs.question: Can I run Galene commercially?
faqs.answer: Yes, the MIT licence permits commercial use, modification and redistribution without royalties.

use_cases.title: University lecture and remote examination platform
use_cases.description: Originally built for and still used to deliver lectures, tutorials and supervised remote exams at Universite Paris Cite and Sorbonne Universite.
use_cases.audience: University IT departments and computer science faculties

use_cases.title: Conference and community event streaming
use_cases.description: Used to host free-software and academic conferences such as LibrePlanet 2024 and JFLA 2021, letting organizers avoid proprietary conferencing platforms.
use_cases.audience: Open-source conference organizers and community groups

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious self-hosted meetings
use_cases.description: Deployed by organizations that want full control over meeting data and infrastructure instead of relying on a third-party video SaaS.

features: Unlimited groups and users when self-hosted
Community support via mailing list and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Galley Solutions is a San Diego-based culinary resource planning (CRP) platform that centralizes recipe, menu, costing and allergen data for restaurants, foodservice operators and dietitians.
enrichment.seo_title: Galley Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Galley Solutions is a San Diego culinary resource planning platform for recipes, costing and allergens. See features, pricing, pros, cons and rivals.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Galley Solutions is a San Diego-based software company that builds what it calls a Culinary Resource Planning, or CRP, platform: a system of record for recipes, menus, costs and nutrition data aimed at restaurants, catering companies, ghost kitchens, hospital and school foodservice operations, and other commercial kitchens. The company was founded in 2017 by Ian Christopher Brigden and Benji Koltai, and its core pitch is that most foodservice businesses run their culinary operations, including recipe development, menu costing, allergen labeling, purchasing and production planning, on a patchwork of spreadsheets, PDFs and disconnected point solutions, which makes it slow and error-prone to answer basic operational questions like the true food cost of a dish or which menu items contain a given allergen. Galley's platform centralizes that data in one place so chefs, operators and dietitians can all work from the same underlying recipe and ingredient records instead of maintaining separate, frequently out-of-sync copies. On the product side, Galley organizes its feature set around three constituencies. For chefs, it provides recipe management and scaling tools that let a dish be documented once and then automatically recalculated for different batch sizes, plating formats or menu contexts. For operators, it provides real-time costing that ties ingredient prices to recipes and menus so margin impact from a supplier price change is visible immediately rather than discovered at month-end reconciliation, along with purchasing, inventory and production-planning workflows. For dietitians and nutrition teams, a notable focus that distinguishes Galley from purely cost-focused kitchen software, it provides automated allergen tracking and nutrition-data tooling, particularly relevant for institutional foodservice operators such as hospital systems, school districts and senior living communities that face regulatory and liability requirements around allergen disclosure. The company reports its platform has been used to create more than 150,000 recipes and has supported the service of more than 5 million meals, and cites operators saving more than 45 hours of administrative work per week after adopting it, though these are company-reported figures rather than independently audited statistics. Galley has raised a total of roughly 20 million dollars in venture funding. The most significant disclosed round was a 14.2 million dollar Series A completed in May 2022, led by Astanor Ventures, a European investor focused on food and agriculture technology, with continued participation from earlier backer Zetta Venture Partners. This funding profile places Galley in the broader foodtech and restaurant-technology investment wave of the early 2020s, alongside adjacent categories like inventory management, food-waste reduction and kitchen automation software. Employee-count estimates vary by source, ranging from roughly 40 to over 60 people depending on the database consulted, consistent with a company operating at Series A or early-growth stage rather than a large enterprise vendor. Galley does not publish self-serve pricing tiers on its website; interested prospects are directed to book a demo or use a signup link tied to a sales conversation, typical for B2B software aimed at mid-market and enterprise foodservice operators rather than single-location independent restaurants. This is a meaningful contrast with lighter-weight, more self-serve competitors, and it signals that Galley's go-to-market is built around consultative sales and implementation support for organizations with more complex, multi-location or multi-brand culinary operations, the kind of customer for whom standardizing hundreds or thousands of recipes across kitchens is a significant, high-value operational project rather than a simple software purchase. The competitive set around Galley includes several categories of adjacent tools. Direct recipe-and-menu-costing competitors include Apicbase, a Belgium-based F and B management platform covering menu engineering, inventory and production; MarginEdge, which focuses heavily on invoice processing and real-time food cost tracking; and meez, a recipe and food-costing platform with a strong culinary-training angle. Broader restaurant back-office and inventory platforms such as CrunchTime and MarketMan compete for some of the same budget, particularly around purchasing and inventory control, though they tend to be less recipe-and-nutrition-centric than Galley. Waste-reduction-focused tools like Winnow occupy an adjacent but distinct niche. Galley's differentiation within this landscape rests on its recipe-first architecture, treating the recipe rather than the invoice or the inventory count as the central object that costing, allergen tracking and production planning all derive from, and on its explicit design for cross-functional use by chefs, operators and dietitians together, rather than serving primarily as a back-office finance tool. The realistic target audience for Galley is mid-size to large foodservice operations: multi-unit restaurant groups, catering and food-production companies, and institutional foodservice providers such as hospitals, universities, senior living and corporate dining that need to manage large, complex recipe libraries with real allergen and nutrition compliance requirements across many kitchens or locations. A single independent restaurant with one menu and one kitchen is less likely to be the ideal customer profile, both because of the platform's evident enterprise-sales orientation and because much of its value, standardizing recipe data across many cooks and locations, compounds with scale. Reviewers on platforms like Capterra rate the product highly, around 4.8 out of 5, consistent with a well-regarded, if narrower and more specialized, tool relative to broader restaurant-operations suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Galley Solutions is a culinary resource planning platform that centralizes recipe, menu, costing, allergen and nutrition data for restaurants, catering companies and institutional foodservice operators.

Founded in 2017 in San Diego by Ian Christopher Brigden and Benji Koltai, Galley has raised about 20 million dollars including a 14.2 million dollar Series A led by Astanor Ventures, and reports over 150,000 recipes created and 5 million-plus meals served on the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers recipe management and scaling, automated menu and recipe costing, allergen and nutrition tracking, and production planning tools designed for chefs, operators and dietitians working from shared data.
It also supports purchasing and inventory integration, multi-unit recipe rollout for restaurant groups, and API-based integrations with existing POS and accounting systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Galley does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers request a demo and receive a custom quote based on the number of locations, recipes and users involved.
This sales-led pricing model is typical for mid-market and enterprise foodservice software and reflects Galley's focus on multi-location operators rather than single-location independent restaurants.

features.title: Recipe management and standardization
features.description: Centralizes recipe data so it can be documented once and reused consistently across a kitchen or organization.

features.title: Automated menu and recipe costing
features.description: Ties live ingredient prices to recipes and menus so margin impact from supplier price changes is visible in real time.

features.title: Allergen and nutrition tracking
features.description: Automates allergen disclosure and nutrition data, particularly valuable for regulated institutional foodservice.

features.title: Recipe scaling
features.description: Automatically recalculates ingredient quantities for different batch sizes and plating formats.

features.title: Inventory and purchasing integration
features.description: Connects recipe and menu data to purchasing and inventory workflows.

features.title: Production planning
features.description: Helps kitchens plan production runs based on recipe and demand data.

features.title: Multi-unit recipe rollout
features.description: Supports standardizing recipes and menus across multiple restaurant locations or brands.

features.title: API and system integrations
features.description: Connects with existing POS and accounting systems to keep data synchronized.

pros: Centralizes recipe, cost and allergen data across chefs, operators and dietitians in one system
Real-time costing helps catch margin erosion from supplier price changes quickly
Backed by significant venture funding (about 20 million dollars) from Astanor Ventures and Zetta Venture Partners
Strong customer traction, with more than 150,000 recipes and 5 million-plus meals served on the platform
Highly rated by users on review platforms like Capterra
cons: No public pricing; requires a sales demo to get a quote
Smaller company than large incumbents like CrunchTime, which may mean a narrower support and integration footprint
Standardizing legacy recipe data during onboarding can take significant time and effort
Primarily geared toward mid-market and enterprise operators, less turnkey for single independent restaurants

faqs.question: What is Galley Solutions?
faqs.answer: Galley Solutions is a culinary resource planning platform that centralizes recipe, menu costing, allergen and nutrition data for restaurants and other commercial kitchens.

faqs.question: How much does Galley cost?
faqs.answer: Galley uses custom, quote-based pricing; prospective customers must book a demo to receive pricing tailored to their number of locations and users.

faqs.question: Who founded Galley Solutions and when?
faqs.answer: Galley was founded in 2017 by Ian Christopher Brigden and Benji Koltai in San Diego, California.

faqs.question: How much funding has Galley raised?
faqs.answer: Galley has raised about 20 million dollars in total funding, including a 14.2 million dollar Series A led by Astanor Ventures with participation from Zetta Venture Partners, completed in May 2022.

faqs.question: Who uses Galley?
faqs.answer: Chefs, multi-unit restaurant operators, catering companies, and institutional foodservice teams such as hospital and school dietitians; the company reports over 150,000 recipes and 5 million-plus meals served through the platform.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Galley?
faqs.answer: Notable alternatives include MarginEdge, Apicbase, CrunchTime, meez and MarketMan.

use_cases.title: Multi-unit restaurant recipe standardization

enrichment.short_description: GameAP is a free, open-source game server management panel written in Go that lets administrators install, configure and monitor game servers such as Minecraft, Counter-Strike 2 and Rust from a single web dashboard.
enrichment.seo_title: GameAP Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GameAP is a free, open-source Go-based game server management panel for Minecraft, CS2 and more. Compare features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GameAP is a free and open-source game server management panel: a self-hosted web application that lets administrators install, configure, start, stop, monitor and update game servers, including Minecraft, Counter-Strike 2, Rust, Grand Theft Auto V and many others, from a browser instead of managing each server by hand over SSH. It occupies the same broad category as tools like Pterodactyl and the newer Pelican panel, and its own marketing explicitly positions it as an alternative to both. The project traces back to roughly 2013 to 2014, when a developer known on GitHub as et-nik (Nikita Kuznetsov) began building a PHP-based Game Admin Panel; that original codebase is preserved on GitHub as a legacy, no-longer-supported version, while the modern GameAP is a from-scratch rewrite maintained under the gameap organization on GitHub. GameAP is not a commercial company; there is no headquarters, funding round or employee count to report, since it is a community open-source project distributed under the permissive MIT licence, which allows both personal and commercial use, including by hosting providers who resell GameAP-based server management as part of a paid product. The defining technical decision behind GameAP is to avoid containerization overhead. Where many modern panels, Pterodactyl for example, run each game server inside a Docker container for isolation, GameAP is built in Go and compiled to a single native binary that runs directly on the host operating system, communicating with a lightweight daemon over gRPC. The project's own claim is that this lets a single dedicated machine host thousands of active game servers using as little as 256 MB of RAM for the panel itself, a meaningfully lower resource bar than container-based alternatives, particularly attractive to budget game-hosting providers and hobbyists running servers on modest VPS instances. The panel supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite or an in-memory database for its own storage, runs on Linux, Windows Server and macOS, and exposes a documented REST API plus WebSocket endpoints for real-time console output, task status and server metrics, making it usable as a backend for a hosting company's own custom customer-facing control panel rather than only as an end-user-facing tool. A distinguishing feature in more recent GameAP releases is a WebAssembly-based plugin architecture, allowing the panel's behavior to be extended using any language that compiles to WASM; the project specifically calls out Go, Rust, C++ and Python as supported options. This is a more flexible extension model than the PHP- or JavaScript-only plugin systems found in many competing panels, and it reflects GameAP's positioning toward technically sophisticated operators, namely hosting companies and power-user communities, rather than casual end users. Day-to-day administrative features include a file manager with archive extraction for editing server files directly in the browser, an RCON-based remote console so admins can send commands and read server output without SSH access, a task scheduler for automating restarts, updates and backups on daily, hourly or custom intervals, and granular per-server, per-user permissions so a hosting provider or community admin can safely delegate limited control to specific users without giving them full server access. Because GameAP is entirely free and open source, there is no pricing model to evaluate in the traditional sense: anyone can clone the repository, run the installer or Docker Compose setup, and operate an unlimited number of managed game servers at no licensing cost, with the only expenses being the underlying server hardware or cloud hosting. The project accepts community support through GitHub and maintains a Patreon page for those who want to fund ongoing development. This puts GameAP in direct competition with Pterodactyl and Pelican, both also free and open source but reliant on Docker containerization, as well as with commercial game-hosting control panels like TCAdmin and Multicraft that charge licensing fees; GameAP's pitch against all of them is lower resource overhead and simpler deployment, at the cost of a smaller community, fewer prebuilt game templates, and less polished documentation than the more established Pterodactyl ecosystem. The realistic audience for GameAP is game server hosting companies looking for white-label management infrastructure, gaming communities and clan administrators running their own dedicated servers, and self-hosting hobbyists who want to manage a handful of game servers on a home server or budget VPS without the memory overhead of a containerized panel. It is less suited to non-technical individual gamers who just want a single click-to-play server, since even with its browser UI, GameAP still assumes comfort with server administration concepts like databases, daemons and, for advanced use, gRPC-based multi-node setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GameAP is a free, open-source, self-hosted web panel for installing, configuring and monitoring game servers such as Minecraft, Counter-Strike 2, Rust and Grand Theft Auto V.

Its roots trace to a PHP-based panel started around 2013-2014 by developer et-nik; the modern GameAP is a full Go rewrite distributed under the MIT licence, positioned as an alternative to Pterodactyl and Pelican.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GameAP runs as a native binary without requiring containers, supporting thousands of servers per node with a REST API, WebSocket events, a file manager and an RCON-based remote console.

A WebAssembly plugin architecture lets developers extend the panel in Go, Rust, C++, Python or any WASM-compilable language, alongside granular per-server user permissions and a task scheduler for automated maintenance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GameAP is completely free under the MIT licence, with no tiers, seat limits, or feature gating; source code can be freely modified and redistributed, including commercially by hosting providers.
The only costs are the server hardware GameAP runs on. Development is community-supported through GitHub contributions and an optional Patreon page.

features.title: Web-based server management
features.description: Install, start, stop and restart game servers from a browser dashboard.

features.title: Remote console access
features.description: Send RCON commands and view live console output without needing SSH access.

features.title: Built-in file manager
features.description: Edit server files and extract archives directly from the web interface.

features.title: Task scheduler
features.description: Automates restarts, updates and backups on daily, hourly or custom intervals.

features.title: Granular per-server permissions
features.description: Assigns fine-grained user access controls on a per-server basis.

features.title: REST API and WebSocket events
features.description: Provides documented API access and real-time events for building custom integrations.

features.title: WASM plugin architecture
features.description: Extends the panel using Go, Rust, C++, Python or any WASM-compilable language.

features.title: Multi-database support
features.description: Works with PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite or an in-memory database backend.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Very lightweight; runs as a native binary without Docker overhead
Flexible WASM-based plugin architecture supporting multiple languages
Cross-platform support for Linux, Windows Server and macOS
Active development with frequent releases on GitHub
cons: Smaller community and GitHub star count than newer competitors like Pterodactyl or Pelican
Documentation and community support historically skew toward Russian-language and Eastern European users
No official managed or hosted cloud version; self-hosting is required
Initial setup involving panel, daemon and gRPC communication is more involved than a fully managed alternative

faqs.question: Is GameAP free?
faqs.answer: Yes, GameAP is released under the MIT license and is free to download, run, and modify, including for commercial use.

faqs.question: What games does GameAP support?
faqs.answer: GameAP supports many dedicated game servers including Minecraft, Counter-Strike 2, Rust and Grand Theft Auto V, among others configurable via its game definitions.

faqs.question: How is GameAP different from Pterodactyl?
faqs.answer: GameAP runs as a native binary without requiring Docker containers, aiming for lower resource overhead, while Pterodactyl is built around per-server Docker containerization.

faqs.question: What tech stack powers GameAP?
faqs.answer: GameAP's backend is written primarily in Go, its frontend uses Vue.js, and the panel communicates with a lightweight daemon over gRPC.

faqs.question: Can I extend GameAP with plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, GameAP supports a WebAssembly-based plugin system that lets developers write extensions in Go, Rust, C++, Python or other WASM-compilable languages.

faqs.question: Who maintains GameAP?
faqs.answer: GameAP is maintained by an open-source community originally started by developer et-nik (Nikita Kuznetsov), with current development at the gameap organization on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Hosting providers running many game servers
use_cases.description: Lets hosting companies manage large fleets of game servers with low resource overhead and white-label control.

use_cases.title: Community and clan server management
use_cases.description: Gives gaming communities a browser-based way to manage their own Minecraft or Counter-Strike 2 servers.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted home lab game servers
use_cases.description: Allows hobbyists to run and manage several game servers on a home server or budget VPS.

features: Full source code under MIT license
Unlimited game servers on your own hardware
Community support on GitHub and Patreon

enrichment.short_description: Gameyfin is a free, open-source, self-hosted game library manager, often described as Jellyfin for games, that scans local game folders, fetches metadata and lets you browse, download and share your collection from a web interface.
enrichment.seo_title: Gameyfin Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gameyfin is a free, open-source self-hosted game library manager, like Jellyfin for games. See features, setup, licensing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Gameyfin is a free and open-source, self-hosted application for organizing and serving a personal video game library, frequently and accurately described by users and reviewers as Jellyfin for games. Just as Jellyfin, Plex or Emby let someone point a media server at a folder of movies and get back a browsable, metadata-rich library, Gameyfin points at folders containing PC game installers or files, sourced from platforms like Steam, Epic Games Store, GOG or a user's own backups, and automatically scans them, fetches cover art and metadata, and presents the result as a clean web interface that can be browsed and downloaded from any device on the network or, depending on configuration, remotely. The project is led by a developer using the handle grimsi and is developed in the open on GitHub under the gameyfin organization, with its first public releases dating to 2023; by mid-2026 the project had reached over 40 tagged releases and version 2.4.0, along with more than 1,000 GitHub stars, indicating a young but fast-moving and increasingly popular project within the self-hosting community. Technically, Gameyfin is built as a modern web application: the backend is written in Kotlin on top of Spring Boot 3, and the frontend combines Vaadin Hilla with React and TypeScript, backed by an embedded H2 database for simplicity of deployment. Extensibility is handled through the PF4J plugin framework, letting the community and the core team add new functionality, such as additional metadata providers, authentication methods or storage integrations, without modifying the core codebase. The project is distributed under the AGPL-3.0 licence, a copyleft licence that requires anyone who modifies Gameyfin and offers it as a network service to also release their modified source code, a common choice for self-hosted community software that wants to prevent commercial forks from closing off improvements. Feature-wise, beyond the core scan-and-serve functionality, Gameyfin supports direct in-browser downloads of games in the library, sharing a library with other users (useful for households or small friend groups pooling a game collection on a home server or NAS), LAN-aware caching so repeated downloads on a local network are fast, and accessibility-minded UI themes including colorblind-friendly options. Authentication is handled through single sign-on via OAuth2 and OpenID Connect, with documented support for self-hosted identity providers popular in the home-lab community such as Authentik and Pocket-ID, letting Gameyfin slot cleanly into an existing self-hosted authentication stack rather than requiring its own separate user database. Deployment is designed to be straightforward: the project ships official Docker images, is available as an app in Unraid's Community Applications store, and can otherwise run on any system with a Java Virtual Machine, giving it broad compatibility across Linux, Windows and macOS hosts commonly used for home servers. Because Gameyfin is entirely open source and, in the project's own words, 100 percent open source and free to use without any paywall, there is no pricing model, subscription or licensing fee to evaluate; the only cost to a user is the hardware they choose to run it on. This places Gameyfin squarely in the same category as other community-funded self-hosted projects rather than venture-backed SaaS, and its closest comparisons are other self-hosted library and media-server tools: Jellyfin and Plex for video, and Romm for game libraries specifically, alongside desktop-only front ends like LaunchBox and Playnite that are not server-based. Gameyfin's particular niche is combining Jellyfin-style server and client architecture, browse and download from any device via a browser rather than a single-machine desktop launcher, with the specific metadata and file-handling needs of PC game installers rather than video files. The target audience is PC gamers and home-lab enthusiasts who already run a self-hosted server, such as a NAS, home server, or small VPS, and want a Netflix-like or Jellyfin-like browsing experience for a personal or family game collection they legally own, rather than a way to acquire new games. It particularly appeals to privacy- and control-conscious users who prefer to manage their own game backups instead of relying solely on platform-specific cloud libraries, and to households that want to share one game collection across multiple family members' computers over a home network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gameyfin is a free, open-source, self-hosted application that scans local folders of PC games, fetches metadata and cover art, and serves the result as a browsable web library, similar to how Jellyfin works for movies and TV.
Led by developer grimsi and first released in 2023, the project has grown quickly, reaching over 40 releases, version 2.4.0, and more than 1,000 GitHub stars by mid-2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Gameyfin automatically indexes game libraries, supports direct in-browser downloads, and lets users share collections with other household members, with LAN-optimized caching for fast local transfers.
It supports single sign-on via OAuth2 and OpenID Connect providers like Authentik and Pocket-ID, a PF4J-based plugin system, and colorblind-accessible themes, and deploys easily via Docker or Unraid.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Gameyfin is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 licence, with no paywalls, subscription tiers, or feature restrictions of any kind.
The only cost is the hardware used to self-host it, such as an existing home server or NAS most users already own.

features.description: Scans configured folders and automatically fetches metadata and cover art for each game.

features.description: Provides a clean browser interface for browsing and directly downloading games in the library.

features.description: Allows multiple users, such as family or friends, to share access to one game library.

features.title: LAN-optimized caching
features.description: Caches data locally to speed up repeated downloads over a home network.

features.title: Single sign-on support
features.description: Integrates with OAuth2 and OpenID Connect identity providers such as Authentik and Pocket-ID.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Uses the PF4J framework to let developers extend Gameyfin without modifying the core codebase.

features.title: Colorblind-accessible themes

features.title: Docker and Unraid deployment
features.description: Ships official Docker images and an Unraid Community Applications listing for simple installation.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paywalls
Actively developed, with over 40 releases and a growing GitHub community
Modern tech stack (Spring Boot, Kotlin, React, TypeScript)
Simple deployment via Docker or Unraid
Supports self-hosted single sign-on integrations
cons: Relatively young project (launched 2023), so some features and polish are still evolving
AGPL-3.0 license imposes copyleft obligations on modified redistributions
Requires users to already own the games they index; does not source game files itself
Smaller plugin and integration ecosystem than established media servers like Jellyfin or Plex

faqs.question: Is Gameyfin free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gameyfin is fully free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license with no paywalls.

faqs.question: What platforms does Gameyfin run on?
faqs.answer: Gameyfin runs via Docker, on Unraid, or on any system with a Java Virtual Machine, covering Linux, Windows and macOS hosts.

faqs.question: Does Gameyfin download games for me?
faqs.answer: No, Gameyfin indexes and serves games you already own and have placed in your library folders; it does not source or distribute game content.

faqs.question: Can multiple people share one Gameyfin library?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gameyfin supports multi-user access so a library can be shared with friends or family.

faqs.question: How does Gameyfin compare to Jellyfin or Plex?
faqs.answer: Jellyfin and Plex are media servers for video and music; Gameyfin applies the same self-hosted library concept specifically to PC game files.

faqs.question: Does Gameyfin support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, through OAuth2 and OpenID Connect, with documented support for providers like Authentik and Pocket-ID.

enrichment.short_description: Garage is a free, open-source, S3-compatible distributed object storage system built in Rust by the Deuxfleurs association, designed to run reliably across small, geographically distributed and even secondhand servers.
enrichment.headquarters: Distributed (Deuxfleurs association members based in Rennes, Paris, Lille and Brussels; registered address in Lille, France)
enrichment.seo_title: Garage Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Garage is a free, open-source S3-compatible object storage system for small, geo-distributed self-hosted clusters. See features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Garage is a free and open-source, S3-compatible distributed object storage system built by Deuxfleurs, a French association of self-hosting enthusiasts and researchers. Where most S3-compatible storage software, including AWS S3 itself and self-hosted alternatives like MinIO, is designed to run inside a single, low-latency data center, Garage was designed from its very first release in 2020 for the opposite scenario: storage clusters made up of nodes running in different physical locations, sometimes with high inter-node network latency, sometimes on heterogeneous or secondhand hardware, and sometimes maintained by volunteers rather than a professional operations team. Deuxfleurs itself was founded by PhD students, including Alex Auvolat and Quentin Dufour, affiliated with Inria's WIDE research team studying distributed systems; Garage grew directly out of that research context and has been used in production by Deuxfleurs to run its own small-scale self-hosted services since its first release. The technical core of Garage combines two ideas from distributed-systems research: a Dynamo-style consistent hashing ring, in the tradition of Amazon's internal Dynamo system, to distribute data across nodes, and Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) to reconcile state between nodes without requiring a consensus protocol like Raft. This is a deliberate architectural choice, since consensus protocols like Raft require a majority of nodes to agree before committing writes, which works well inside a single data center with low, predictable latency but degrades badly when nodes are spread across cities or countries with variable network conditions. By avoiding that consensus bottleneck, Garage lets every node handle read and write requests independently and reconcile eventual consistency afterward, which is what allows it to stay available and responsive even when some nodes are slow to reach or entirely unreachable, a property the project describes as being reliable enough to run outside datacenters. Garage is written in Rust and ships as a single, dependency-free static binary, keeping deployment simple: there is no external database, no separate metadata service and no complex orchestration layer required, and the project documents minimum requirements as low as 1GB of RAM, 16GB of disk and a CPU from the last decade (x86_64, ARMv7 or ARMv8), explicitly supporting the kind of secondhand or repurposed hardware that budget-constrained self-hosters and community tech collectives often run. On the API side, Garage implements the core of the Amazon S3 REST API, including multipart uploads for large files, presigned URLs, bucket policies, CORS configuration and object versioning. Because this is the same API surface used by Amazon S3 and other S3-compatible systems, existing tools and applications generally work with Garage without modification, including popular self-hosted software like Nextcloud for file storage, Matrix for chat media, Mastodon and PeerTube for federated social and video platforms, as well as generic S3 clients and backup tools like rclone, restic and Cyberduck. This compatibility is a major part of Garage's practical value: rather than asking developers to learn a new storage API, it lets them swap Garage in as a drop-in replacement for any part of their stack that already speaks S3. Garage is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPLv3), a strong copyleft licence that requires anyone who runs a modified version of Garage as a network service to publish their modifications, a common choice for infrastructure software built by non-commercial collectives that want to prevent proprietary forks. Unlike most tools in the comparable open-source infrastructure space, Garage's ongoing development has not been funded by venture capital or a company but primarily by European public research and internet-infrastructure funding programs: NGI POINTER, NLnet's NGI0 Entrust programme and the NGI0 Commons Fund all supported Garage's development between 2021 and 2025, reflecting the European Commission's Next Generation Internet initiative, which funds public-interest, non-commercial internet infrastructure projects. As an association rather than a company, Deuxfleurs does not report an employee count or a single headquarters in the conventional sense; its members are spread across several French and Belgian cities including Rennes, Paris, Lille and Brussels, with the association's formal registered address in Lille. Because there is no company selling Garage, there is no pricing model to describe beyond free; anyone can download and run it at zero licensing cost, with the only expense being whatever hardware they choose to run it on. Its closest competitors are other self-hostable object storage systems: MinIO is the most direct comparison and is far more widely deployed and commercially supported, including a paid enterprise tier, but is architected primarily for high-performance, low-latency, single-site or tightly-networked multi-site deployments rather than Garage's geo-distributed, high-latency-tolerant design; SeaweedFS and Ceph's RGW object gateway are other alternatives, generally aimed at larger-scale, more operationally complex deployments than Garage's small-to-medium sweet spot. Garage's realistic target audience is self-hosters, small non-profit and community tech collectives, and operators of federated services such as Mastodon instances, Matrix homeservers and small PeerTube instances, who need reliable, S3-compatible storage across a handful of low-cost or donated servers rather than a professionally managed data center footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Garage is a free, open-source, S3-compatible object storage system built by the Deuxfleurs association and released for production use starting in 2020, purpose-built for small, geographically distributed self-hosted clusters.

Unlike storage systems designed for a single data center, Garage uses Dynamo-style consistent hashing and CRDTs so nodes can handle requests independently even under high inter-node latency, avoiding the consensus bottlenecks of Raft-based systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Garage implements the core Amazon S3 REST API, including multipart uploads, presigned URLs, bucket policies, CORS and versioning, making it a drop-in backend for tools like Nextcloud, Matrix, Mastodon and PeerTube.
It ships as a single dependency-free Rust binary that runs on as little as 1GB of RAM and supports heterogeneous or secondhand x86_64, ARMv7 and ARMv8 hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Garage is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, with no company, subscription or licensing fee behind it; anyone can run it at zero cost beyond their own hardware.
Development has instead been funded through European public research programs such as NGI POINTER and NLnet's NGI0 Entrust and Commons Fund initiatives.

features.title: Amazon S3 API compatibility
features.description: Supports multipart uploads, presigned URLs, bucket policies, CORS and versioning for drop-in compatibility with S3 tooling.

features.title: Dynamo-style consistent hashing
features.description: Distributes data across nodes using a hashing ring inspired by Amazon's Dynamo system.

features.title: CRDT-based consistency
features.description: Reconciles state between nodes without a consensus protocol, avoiding bottlenecks under high latency.

features.title: Geo-distributed replication
features.description: Designed to replicate data across nodes in different physical locations and stay available if some are unreachable.

features.title: Single dependency-free binary
features.description: Ships as one static Rust binary with no external database or orchestration required.

features.title: Low hardware requirements
features.description: Runs on as little as 1GB RAM and 16GB disk, supporting older or secondhand machines.

features.title: Broad ecosystem compatibility
features.description: Works with Nextcloud, Matrix, Mastodon, PeerTube, rclone, restic and other S3-compatible tools.

features.title: High failure resilience
features.description: Built to tolerate node, disk and network failures without losing availability.

pros: Free and open source under AGPLv3 with no vendor lock-in
Genuinely lightweight; runs on secondhand or low-power hardware
Purpose-built for geo-distributed, non-datacenter deployments
Avoids consensus bottlenecks through a CRDT-based design
Backed by public research funding rather than venture capital, aligning incentives with users
cons: Not a fully managed service; requires self-hosting and cluster administration
Smaller ecosystem and less commercial support than AWS S3, MinIO or Backblaze B2
Best suited to small-to-medium scale rather than hyperscale storage needs
AGPLv3 licensing can be a blocker for some commercial redistribution use cases

faqs.question: Is Garage really S3-compatible?
faqs.answer: Yes, it implements the core Amazon S3 REST API including multipart uploads, presigned URLs, CORS and bucket policies, so most existing S3 clients work unmodified.

faqs.question: Who builds and maintains Garage?
faqs.answer: The Deuxfleurs association, a French collective of self-hosting enthusiasts and researchers, originally founded by PhD students including Alex Auvolat and Quentin Dufour.

faqs.question: How is Garage funded?
faqs.answer: Through European public research funding programs including NGI POINTER, NLnet's NGI0 Entrust, and the NGI0 Commons Fund, between 2021 and 2025.

faqs.question: What hardware does Garage need?
faqs.answer: As little as 1GB RAM and 16GB disk per node, running on x86_64, ARMv7 or ARMv8 CPUs, including older or secondhand machines.

faqs.question: What applications work with Garage?
faqs.answer: Nextcloud, Matrix, Mastodon, PeerTube, rclone and any other S3-API-compatible software.

faqs.question: How does Garage differ from MinIO?
faqs.answer: Garage is purpose-built for small, geographically distributed clusters with high network latency tolerance and CRDT-based consistency, while MinIO focuses more on high-performance, low-latency data center deployments.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted backup target
use_cases.description: Provides an S3-compatible destination for backup tools like restic and rclone across low-cost servers.

use_cases.title: Storage backend for federated apps
use_cases.description: Serves as the object storage layer for federated services such as Mastodon, Matrix and PeerTube.

use_cases.title: Geo-redundant community storage
use_cases.description: Lets non-profits and collectives replicate data across volunteer or donated hardware in different locations.

features: Unlimited nodes and storage on your own hardware
Community documentation and chat support channels

enrichment.short_description: GatherUp is a customer feedback and online reputation management platform that helps local businesses and multi-location brands collect reviews, run NPS surveys and monitor their presence across 100-plus review sites.
enrichment.seo_title: GatherUp Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GatherUp is a review and reputation management platform for local businesses. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GatherUp is a customer feedback, reviews and online reputation management platform aimed at local businesses, multi-location brands and the marketing agencies that serve them. Founded in San Jose, California in 2013 by a group of local search marketers, the company built its product around a straightforward but high-value workflow for local businesses: systematically ask customers for feedback right after a purchase or service interaction, route happy customers toward public reviews on sites like Google, and route unhappy customers into a private feedback loop the business can resolve before it becomes a public one-star review. In 2019, GatherUp was acquired by Traject, a marketing-technology holding company now headquartered in Austin, Texas, that also owns other local-marketing and social-media tools including Grade.us, Loomly, PLANOLY and Sendible. Under Traject's ownership, GatherUp has continued to operate as a distinct product and brand rather than being folded into a combined suite, and reported employee counts for the GatherUp team specifically range from roughly half a dozen to a few dozen depending on the data source, consistent with a mature, profitable product line inside a larger portfolio company rather than an independently scaling venture-funded startup. The core of GatherUp's platform is review and feedback generation: automated email and SMS requests sent to customers, configurable to route feedback based on sentiment, paired with monitoring across more than 100 review sites so a business can see, and via AI-assisted response suggestions reply to, new reviews as they appear across Google, Facebook and industry-specific directories without manually checking each site. Beyond basic review generation, GatherUp includes Net Promoter Score (NPS) survey tooling for measuring and tracking customer sentiment over time, QR codes and custom feedback links for capturing feedback in physical locations, review widgets and automated social sharing so positive reviews can be redistributed as marketing content, and API access plus customer-list upload integrations so the review-request workflow can be triggered from a business's existing CRM or point-of-sale system. Two notable paid add-ons extend the core platform: Listings Hub, which syncs a business's name, address and phone information across roughly 125 online directories to keep local SEO listings consistent, and Review Defense, a tiered service focused on detecting and disputing fraudulent or policy-violating reviews. GatherUp's pricing is subscription-based and scales with the number of business locations being managed. The entry-level Small Business plan is priced at 99 dollars per month for a single location and includes 300 SMS credits and 3,000 email credits per month, full 100-plus site review monitoring, NPS surveys and AI-generated review response suggestions; a 14-day free trial is commonly offered to let prospective customers evaluate the platform before committing. For businesses managing multiple locations, pricing drops on a per-location basis; reported figures show plans starting around 60 dollars per location per month for the 2-10 location tier, with further volume discounts available down to roughly 25 dollars per location per month for very large multi-location portfolios of 100-plus locations, reflecting the platform's dual focus on both single-location small businesses and large franchise or multi-brand operators. Agencies that want to resell reputation management services to their own clients under their own branding can access a separate white-label Agency plan, priced on a custom quote basis rather than published rate cards. GatherUp's competitive set spans several overlapping categories of reputation-management and customer-experience software. Birdeye and Podium are its most direct competitors, both offering broadly similar review generation, messaging and reputation-monitoring feature sets aimed at the same local-business and franchise market; Reputation.com competes more at the large-enterprise end of the market with heavier emphasis on social listening and broader brand reputation; and Yext competes primarily on the listings-management side that GatherUp addresses through its Listings Hub add-on. Within the Traject portfolio itself, Grade.us serves a similar reputation-management use case and is positioned more toward marketing agencies specifically, giving Traject two related but differently-targeted products in the same broad category. The realistic target audience for GatherUp spans single-location small businesses that want a straightforward, done-for-you review generation workflow; multi-location brands and franchise groups that need centralized reputation reporting across dozens or hundreds of locations with per-location cost efficiency; and marketing agencies that want to offer white-label reputation management as a service line to their own client base. Businesses primarily looking for the cheapest possible entry point may find GatherUp's 99 dollar per month starting price higher than some lighter-weight review-request tools, but the trade-off is a more complete feature set spanning surveys, listings and fraud-detection add-ons within a single connected platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GatherUp is a customer feedback and reputation management platform founded in San Jose in 2013 and acquired by Traject in 2019, helping local businesses and multi-location brands collect reviews and monitor their online reputation.

The platform routes customer feedback through automated requests, directing happy customers toward public reviews and unhappy customers into a private resolution loop, while monitoring more than 100 review sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include automated email and SMS review requests, NPS surveys, AI-generated review response suggestions, QR codes and feedback links, and review widgets for social sharing.
Paid add-ons extend the platform further, including Listings Hub for business-listing syncing across roughly 125 directories and Review Defense for detecting and disputing fraudulent reviews.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GatherUp starts at 99 dollars per month for a single location on the Small Business plan, with a 14-day free trial commonly offered.
Multi-location pricing drops to roughly 60 dollars per location per month for 2-10 locations, with further volume discounts at scale, plus a custom-quoted white-label Agency plan for resellers.

faqs.answer: Plans start around 99 dollars per month for a single location, with multi-location pricing around 60 dollars per location per month depending on volume, and custom Agency pricing for white-label resellers.

faqs.question: Who owns GatherUp?
faqs.answer: GatherUp was founded in 2013 and acquired by Traject, a marketing-technology holding company, in 2019.

faqs.answer: Yes, a 14-day free trial is commonly offered to prospective customers.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to GatherUp?
