enrichment.short_description: Gatus is a developer-oriented health dashboard and status page tool, available as free self-hosted open-source software or as a managed cloud service at gatus.io, that monitors HTTP, TCP, DNS and other endpoints and alerts you when they fail.
enrichment.seo_title: Gatus Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gatus is a free, open-source developer status page and health dashboard with 40+ alert integrations. See features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Gatus is a developer-oriented health monitoring and status page tool that exists in two forms: a free, open-source, self-hosted project on GitHub, maintained primarily by a developer known by the handle TwiN, and a managed cloud version offered commercially at gatus.io for teams that would rather not operate their own monitoring infrastructure. The open-source project dates to 2019 and has grown into one of the more popular self-hosted monitoring tools in its category, with more than 10,000 GitHub stars and hundreds of forks by mid-2026. Unlike traditional application performance monitoring tools that passively observe real user traffic, Gatus takes an active-checking approach: it is configured with a list of endpoints, including HTTP and HTTPS URLs, TCP ports, DNS records, ICMP ping targets, WebSocket, gRPC, SSH connections, or TLS and STARTTLS certificates, and periodically probes each one, evaluating the result against a set of conditions the operator defines, such as an expected HTTP status code, a maximum response time, specific text in the response body, or a minimum number of days before a TLS certificate expires. When a condition fails, Gatus can trigger alerts through more than 40 built-in integrations, including Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, PagerDuty, Twilio for SMS and voice, Google Chat, Datadog, Splunk and generic custom webhooks, in addition to maintaining a public or internal status page and exporting metrics in Prometheus format for teams that already run a Grafana and Prometheus observability stack. The design philosophy behind Gatus is explicitly configuration as code: the entire monitoring setup, meaning which endpoints to check, how often, which conditions define health, and where alerts should go, lives in a single YAML file or a set of YAML files that Gatus automatically merges, with support for environment-variable interpolation so secrets like webhook URLs or API tokens do not need to be hardcoded. This stands in contrast to some competing tools, most notably Uptime Kuma, which emphasizes a fully point-and-click web UI for configuring checks; teams that prefer to version-control their monitoring configuration alongside their infrastructure-as-code workflows, such as Terraform, Kubernetes manifests, or GitOps pipelines, tend to gravitate toward Gatus's YAML-first model, while teams that want a non-technical staff member to add a check through a UI without touching a config file may prefer Uptime Kuma or a similar tool. Gatus supports several more advanced monitoring patterns beyond simple single-endpoint checks: Suites allow sequential, workflow-style checks useful for verifying a multi-step process like a login flow still works end to end, External Endpoints let agents running behind private networks or NAT push their status into a central Gatus instance rather than requiring Gatus to reach them directly, and Announcements let operators post incident updates directly on the public status page. Deployment options reflect Gatus's developer-first audience: official Docker images are published to both GitHub Container Registry and Docker Hub, a Helm chart is available for Kubernetes deployments, Terraform modules exist for infrastructure-as-code rollouts, and a plain compiled binary is available for teams that prefer to run it directly on a VM. Because Gatus has minimal runtime dependencies and can use lightweight storage backends, self-hosting costs are typically very low; community write-ups commonly cite running Gatus plus a small PostgreSQL database on platforms like Railway for around 2 to 5 dollars a month, which compares favorably to dedicated commercial status-page products like Better Uptime, whose plans commonly start around 29 dollars a month and often charge per monitored endpoint. This makes the self-hosted, Apache 2.0-licensed core of Gatus effectively free for unlimited endpoints and alert rules, with the operator's only real cost being their own server time and a small hosting bill. For organizations that want the Gatus experience without operating the underlying infrastructure themselves, gatus.io offers a managed, hosted version of the product: users can start exploring the dashboard for free and then move to a paid account tier to publish a live public status page and unlock additional account-level features, though gatus.io does not publish a fully itemized public rate card in the way some competitors do, so exact current pricing is best confirmed directly on the gatus.io pricing page at the time of purchase. This dual-track model, a genuinely free, unlimited, self-hostable open-source core plus an optional paid managed convenience layer, mirrors the strategy used by other successful open-source infrastructure projects and lets Gatus serve both budget-conscious self-hosters and teams that prioritize convenience over control. Gatus's direct competitors span both the self-hosted open-source space and the commercial SaaS space. In self-hosted, open-source monitoring, its closest peers are Uptime Kuma, with UI-first configuration and broad community adoption, and Upptime, a GitHub-Actions-based, git-native status page approach. In commercial SaaS status pages and uptime monitoring, it competes for budget with Statuspage from Atlassian, Better Uptime, Pingdom and UptimeRobot, though those tools generally charge per-monitor or per-seat fees that Gatus's self-hosted core avoids entirely. The realistic target audience is developers, DevOps and SRE teams who want infrastructure-as-code-style monitoring configuration, teams running Kubernetes or other container-orchestrated environments who want a lightweight status page that fits their existing GitOps workflow, and self-hosting hobbyists monitoring personal infrastructure who want a free alternative to paid uptime-monitoring SaaS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gatus is a developer-oriented health dashboard and status page tool, maintained as a free, open-source project since 2019 by a developer known as TwiN, with a managed cloud version also offered at gatus.io.
It actively probes HTTP, TCP, DNS, ICMP, WebSocket, gRPC, SSH and TLS endpoints on a schedule and evaluates configurable conditions to determine health, rather than passively observing real traffic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Gatus supports more than 40 alerting integrations including Slack, Teams, Discord, PagerDuty and Twilio, along with Prometheus metrics export and public or internal status pages.
Advanced features include Suites for sequential workflow checks, External Endpoints for agents behind private networks, and Announcements for posting incident updates directly to a status page.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The self-hosted core of Gatus is free under the Apache 2.0 license, with unlimited endpoints and alert rules; typical self-hosting costs run only a few dollars a month on platforms like Railway.
Gatus.io offers a managed cloud version with a free exploration tier and paid account plans for teams that want a hosted status page without running their own infrastructure.

features.title: Multi-protocol health checks
features.description: Monitors HTTP/HTTPS, ICMP, TCP, DNS, WebSocket, gRPC, SSH, STARTTLS, TLS and GraphQL endpoints.

features.title: Condition-based evaluation
features.description: Evaluates checks against configurable conditions such as status code, response time, body content and certificate expiry.

features.title: 40-plus alerting integrations
features.description: Sends alerts to Slack, Teams, Discord, PagerDuty, Twilio, Datadog and other providers out of the box.

features.title: YAML-based configuration
features.description: Defines the entire monitoring setup as version-controllable YAML with environment variable interpolation.

features.title: Public status pages
features.description: Publishes uptime and response-time badges and public-facing status dashboards.

features.title: Suites for workflow checks
features.description: Runs sequential, multi-step checks to verify end-to-end flows like a login process.

features.title: External Endpoints
features.description: Lets agents behind private networks push their status into a central Gatus instance.

features.title: Prometheus metrics export
features.description: Exports monitoring data in Prometheus format for existing observability stacks.

pros: Free, unlimited-endpoint self-hosted option under the Apache 2.0 license
Very lightweight and inexpensive to self-host
Huge range of built-in alert integrations (40-plus)
Simple, version-controllable YAML configuration
Large, active GitHub community with 10,000-plus stars
cons: Self-hosted version requires running and maintaining your own infrastructure
Managed gatus.io plans add cost on top of the otherwise-free core project
UI and enterprise features like SSO and advanced role-based access are less mature than dedicated commercial status-page vendors
Documentation is community-maintained and can lag behind the newest features

faqs.question: Is Gatus free?
faqs.answer: The core Gatus project is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted at no cost; gatus.io also offers a paid managed hosting option for teams that do not want to run their own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who created Gatus?
faqs.answer: It was created and is primarily maintained by a developer known as TwiN, with contributions from the open-source community.

faqs.question: What can Gatus monitor?
faqs.answer: HTTP/HTTPS endpoints, ICMP ping, TCP ports, DNS records, WebSocket, gRPC, SSH, TLS/STARTTLS certificate expiry and GraphQL endpoints.

faqs.question: How is Gatus different from Uptime Kuma?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, self-hosted monitoring tools; Gatus is configuration-file (YAML) driven and Go-based, while Uptime Kuma emphasizes a fully UI-driven setup.

faqs.question: Does Gatus support Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be deployed via Docker, Kubernetes Helm charts, or Terraform, in addition to a plain binary.

faqs.question: Can Gatus send alerts to Slack or PagerDuty?
faqs.answer: Yes, alongside 40-plus other providers including Teams, Discord, Twilio and custom webhooks.

use_cases.title: Internal service and API uptime monitoring
use_cases.description: Actively checks internal services and APIs and alerts engineering teams when they fail.

use_cases.title: Public status pages for SaaS products
use_cases.description: Gives SaaS companies a way to communicate uptime and incidents to customers.
use_cases.audience: SaaS companies communicating uptime to customers

use_cases.title: Home lab infrastructure monitoring
use_cases.description: Lets self-hosting hobbyists monitor personal servers and services for free.

features: Hosted status pages with no infrastructure to manage

enrichment.short_description: Gem is an AI-powered recruiting platform, often described as a Salesforce for hiring, that combines a sourcing CRM, applicant tracking, scheduling and analytics to help talent acquisition teams find, engage and hire candidates.
enrichment.seo_title: Gem Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gem is an AI recruiting CRM and ATS platform used by 1,200+ talent teams. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Gem is an AI-powered talent acquisition platform, commonly described in the market as 'a Salesforce for hiring' or 'a CRM for recruiting,' built to help talent acquisition teams source, engage, track and analyze candidates throughout the hiring process. This is the recruiting-software company at gem.com, headquartered in San Francisco, and should not be confused with an unrelated cryptocurrency and NFT company that also used the Gem name before being acquired by Blockdaemon; the two are entirely separate businesses. Gem the recruiting platform was founded in 2017 by Steven Bartel and Nick Bushak, both of whom had previously worked as engineering managers at Dropbox and Facebook, with Bartel currently serving as CEO. Their founding thesis was that recruiting teams, especially at fast-growing technology companies, lacked the kind of dedicated CRM tooling that sales teams had long taken for granted in products like Salesforce, and instead were forced to track sourcing and candidate relationships in spreadsheets or repurposed applicant tracking systems that were not built for proactive, high-volume outbound sourcing. Gem has raised a total of 148 million dollars in venture funding to date. The company's most significant and widely reported round was a 100 million dollar Series C completed in September 2021, led by ICONIQ Growth with continued participation from earlier investors Greylock Partners and Accel, plus new investors Sapphire Ventures and Meritech Capital; that round valued Gem at 1.2 billion dollars, making it a unicorn. Other investors associated with the company across its funding history include Cisco Investments, M12 (Microsoft's venture arm), Notable Capital, Team8, IBM Ventures and a number of individual angel investors. Employee-count estimates vary meaningfully by data source, ranging from roughly 150 to over 500, which likely reflects both genuine headcount changes over time and inconsistent methodology between data providers; a reasonable working estimate is a company in the low-to-mid hundreds of employees as of 2026. Functionally, Gem is built around four connected workflows. Sourcing and CRM tooling gives recruiters access to a database the company describes as covering more than 800 million professional profiles, combined with browser-based tools, including a well-known Chrome extension that integrates directly with LinkedIn Recruiter, that let sourcers save candidates, see engagement history, and launch personalized, multi-step outreach sequences across email without leaving the pages they are already browsing. Applicant tracking functionality covers job requisition management, structured application review, interview feedback collection and offer management, either as Gem's own native ATS or layered on top of an existing ATS like Greenhouse or Lever depending on which deployment model a customer chooses. Scheduling tools handle interview coordination, including a self-service candidate scheduling portal and automated interviewer load-balancing to spread interview volume evenly across a hiring panel. Analytics tools provide pipeline funnel dashboards, hiring forecasts and benchmarking data that let talent acquisition leaders compare their team's metrics, such as time-to-fill and source effectiveness, against industry data, an increasingly important selling point as recruiting organizations are asked to justify headcount and demonstrate return on investment to finance and executive stakeholders. More recently, Gem has layered AI agents on top of these workflows, including AI-assisted sourcing that suggests and ranks candidates, AI-assisted application review, and talent rediscovery features that resurface previously sourced or previously applied candidates who might fit a new opening, reducing the amount of cold, from-scratch sourcing a team needs to do for every new role. Gem's pricing is largely custom and quote-based, priced per seat and scaled to company size and deployment model, with two primary architectural choices offered to customers: Gem plus your ATS, where Gem operates as a sourcing and CRM layer that connects to an existing applicant tracking system, or Gem All-in-One, where Gem itself serves as the full ATS as well as the CRM. Within either model, Gem distinguishes between a Standard Seat, giving access to core sourcing, ATS workflows and candidate management, and an AI-Powered Seat, which unlocks the AI sourcing, application-review and candidate-ranking agents on top of the standard feature set, implying the more advanced AI functionality carries an additional per-seat cost. The one publicly disclosed pricing point is a startup-focused tier: companies with 1 to 10 full-time employees can access Gem's AI sourcing, CRM, analytics, scheduling and ATS functionality for 130 dollars per month, discounted from a standard rate of 270 dollars per month, with an additional 10 percent discount for annual billing; Gem also offers steeper startup incentives, including up to six months free followed by 50 percent off for companies under 30 employees, and reduced rates from day one for companies with 30 to 100 employees, reflecting a deliberate strategy of acquiring venture-backed startups early and growing revenue with them as they scale into larger, full-price enterprise customers. Gem serves more than 1,200 talent acquisition teams, with disclosed enterprise customers including Wayfair, Dropbox, Nasdaq, Cisco and DoorDash, positioning it firmly in the mid-market-to-enterprise segment of recruiting software rather than competing primarily on price with lightweight tools built for very small teams. Its most direct competitors are Ashby, a fast-growing challenger that bundles ATS, scheduling and analytics into a single modern product and is often discussed as a combined Greenhouse and Lever plus Gem replacement; the more established enterprise incumbents Greenhouse and Lever, which compete more on ATS process and compliance than on proactive sourcing; sourcing-focused rivals hireEZ and SeekOut, which compete most directly with Gem's CRM and AI-sourcing functionality; and Beamery, which positions itself more broadly as a Talent Lifecycle Management platform extending into workforce planning and internal mobility beyond pure recruiting CRM. Gem's differentiation within this set rests on its deep, long-standing LinkedIn, Gmail and Outlook workflow integration, its flexibility to sit alongside rather than replace an existing ATS, and its emphasis on recruiting analytics and benchmarking for talent acquisition leadership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gem is an AI-powered recruiting CRM and applicant tracking platform founded in 2017 by former Dropbox and Facebook engineering managers Steven Bartel and Nick Bushak, headquartered in San Francisco.

The company has raised 148 million dollars in total funding, including a 100 million dollar Series C in 2021 at a 1.2 billion dollar unicorn valuation, and now serves more than 1,200 talent acquisition teams including Wayfair, Dropbox, Cisco and DoorDash.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Gem combines sourcing and CRM tools backed by more than 800 million candidate profiles, applicant tracking, interview scheduling with load balancing, and pipeline analytics and benchmarking for talent acquisition leaders.
Newer AI-powered features include AI sourcing agents, AI-assisted application review, and talent rediscovery that resurfaces previously sourced candidates for new openings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Gem uses largely custom, per-seat pricing that varies by company size and deployment model, distinguishing between Standard Seats and AI-Powered Seats.

The one public pricing point is a startup tier at 130 dollars per month for companies with 1-10 employees, with additional discounts, including up to six months free, for companies under 30 or 30-100 employees.

features.title: Talent sourcing CRM
features.description: Gives recruiters access to a database of more than 800 million candidate profiles for proactive sourcing.

features.title: Multi-channel automated outreach
features.description: Launches personalized, multi-step email outreach sequences to candidates.

features.title: Applicant tracking system
features.description: Manages job requisitions, application review, interview feedback and offers, natively or alongside an existing ATS.

features.title: Interview scheduling
features.description: Provides a self-service candidate scheduling portal with automated interviewer load balancing.

features.title: Pipeline analytics and benchmarking
features.description: Delivers funnel dashboards, hiring forecasts and industry benchmark comparisons for TA leaders.

features.title: AI sourcing and ranking agents
features.description: Uses AI to suggest, rank and rediscover candidates for open roles.

features.title: LinkedIn, Gmail and Outlook integration
features.description: Integrates directly with LinkedIn Recruiter and email clients via a browser extension.

features.title: Flexible deployment model
features.description: Works alongside an existing ATS like Greenhouse or Lever, or as an all-in-one replacement.

pros: Deep integration with LinkedIn Recruiter, Gmail and Outlook streamlines sourcing workflows
Strong analytics and benchmarking for measuring recruiting performance
Flexible deployment; can sit alongside an existing ATS or replace it entirely
Well-funded (148 million dollars raised) with enterprise customers like Wayfair, Dropbox, Cisco and DoorDash
Discounted startup pricing tier lowers the barrier for small companies
cons: Full pricing is largely custom and quote-based rather than transparent for larger teams
AI-powered features require a separate, likely pricier seat tier on top of standard seats
Can be more sourcing and CRM-focused than some all-in-one competitors like Ashby
Best value is realized at scale, so very small non-startup teams may find it costly relative to lighter tools

faqs.question: Who founded Gem and when?
faqs.answer: Gem was founded in 2017 by Steven Bartel and Nick Bushak, both former engineering managers at Dropbox and Facebook.

faqs.question: How much funding has Gem raised?
faqs.answer: Gem has raised a total of 148 million dollars, including a 100 million dollar Series C in September 2021 led by ICONIQ Growth at a 1.2 billion dollar valuation.

faqs.question: How much does Gem cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is largely custom based on company size and chosen deployment; a discounted startup tier starts around 130 dollars per month for companies with 1-10 employees, with additional discounts for companies under 30 or 30-100 employees.

faqs.question: Does Gem replace my existing ATS?
faqs.answer: It can either integrate alongside an existing ATS like Greenhouse or Lever, or serve as a full all-in-one ATS and CRM replacement.

faqs.question: What companies use Gem?
faqs.answer: Enterprise customers include Wayfair, Dropbox, Nasdaq, Cisco and DoorDash, among more than 1,200 talent acquisition teams.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Gem?
faqs.answer: Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, hireEZ, SeekOut and Beamery.

use_cases.title: Proactive candidate sourcing at scale
use_cases.description: Helps recruiters build and manage large outbound sourcing pipelines with CRM-style tracking.
use_cases.audience: Technical and executive recruiters and sourcers

use_cases.title: Recruiting analytics and forecasting
use_cases.description: Gives talent acquisition leadership visibility into pipeline health, forecasts and benchmarks.
use_cases.audience: Talent acquisition leaders and recruiting operations teams

use_cases.title: Lean startup hiring
use_cases.description: Provides discounted access to sourcing, ATS and analytics tools for early-stage companies.

features: Gem All-in-One or Gem plus existing ATS
